Q1: I tried to keep things consistent, honestly. Being 25 and juggling everything, I carved out study blocks mostly in the evenings after work and responsibilities. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often listen to recorded lectures while doing other things, then sit down for focused study about 26 hours a week. I kept a loose schedule on my phone, but life doesn't always cooperate with plans.
Q2: My pattern varied quite a bit depending on what was due. I aimed for regular evening sessions, but when deadlines hit, everything shifted into crisis mode. Some weeks I'd be steady, others I'd cram. With 12 online courses to manage, I had to be flexible or I'd drown. Deadlines definitely dictated my rhythm more than I'd like to admit.
Q3: Usually it was checking if something new was posted or if a deadline was coming up. I'd get notifications on my phone and that would prompt me to log in. Sometimes I logged in just to see discussion posts from peers since that peer support really helped me stay connected. It became almost like checking social media, just part of my daily scroll.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks it became automatic. I'd wake up, check my phone, log into the LMS during breakfast. The discussions kept me coming back because I actually enjoyed the peer interactions. Having 17 posts and connecting with about 25 classmates made it feel less like a chore and more like a community thing.
Q5: The video lectures were huge for me since I learn best by listening. I watched over 112 minutes of video content because hearing explanations just clicks better for me than reading. The discussion forums were also valuable because bouncing ideas off peers helped concepts stick. When someone explained something their way, it often made more sense than the textbook.
Q6: I'll be honest, I often skimmed or ignored the dense reading materials. With low resources, I couldn't always print things out, and reading on screens for hours hurt my eyes. Some supplementary PDFs just sat there untouched. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, it usually got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to the exam or final grade. Also, if it involved interaction with others, I was more invested. The peer discussions felt valuable because they weren't just busywork. If I could listen to something or talk through it with someone, I was engaged.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford time-wise. Required ones got priority because they affected my grade directly. With my assignment completion being low, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy. Optional stuff sometimes felt interesting but also felt like a trap that would eat into time I needed elsewhere.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded lectures and listened to them on my commute or while cooking. I'd take notes by hand and study from those instead of being logged in. A lot of my learning happened with headphones in, away from the computer. My LMS activity wouldn't capture any of that audio-based studying I did offline.
Q10: There were many nights I studied from handwritten notes or replayed downloaded audio files without touching the LMS. I'd discuss concepts with a study buddy over the phone, working through problems verbally. Those conversations were some of my best learning moments, but they left zero digital footprint. The system only saw silence.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video lecture once, then listen to it three or four more times offline. Same with any audio resources. I didn't have unlimited data, so downloading once and studying repeatedly was more practical. The LMS would show one access, but I got way more mileage out of those materials.
Q12: There were stretches where I felt really sad and disconnected. Life stuff piled up, money was tight, and motivation just tanked. My attendance dropped during those periods, and even logging in felt exhausting. The peer support helped pull me back, but there were definitely dark weeks where engagement felt impossible.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about bills, the LMS became just another demand I couldn't meet. Confusion in course material made me avoid certain modules entirely. I'd open something, feel overwhelmed, and close it. Competing responsibilities always won against optional course activities.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd scroll through materials but nothing was sinking in. I'd watch a video but realize I hadn't absorbed anything because my mind was elsewhere. Those sessions looked productive on paper but were basically empty. Being physically present online didn't mean I was mentally engaged.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, which is why my completion rate was low. Sometimes the task felt too big and I'd freeze up. Other times I was dealing with sadness and just couldn't muster the energy. I knew they were important, but knowing and doing are different things when you're struggling.
Q16: Deadlines were my main driver, honestly. I'd ramp up LMS activity in the days before something was due. Without a deadline, I'd drift. The structure of due dates kept me from completely falling off, even if it meant last-minute cramming. My login frequency spiked around deadline weeks.
Q17: When I got feedback, it motivated me to engage more, especially positive feedback. But when feedback was delayed or harsh, I'd withdraw a bit. Knowing someone actually looked at my work made me feel like the effort mattered. The peer support aspect helped too, getting encouragement from classmates kept me going.
Q18: The course structure with its modules and deadlines gave me a framework to follow. Without that, I would've been lost. Having clear milestones helped me pace myself, even if I didn't always hit them perfectly. The discussion requirements also forced me to engage when I might have otherwise stayed silent.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier since it fit my learning style. But some modules were text-heavy, which was harder for me. When navigation was confusing or resources were buried in subfolders, I'd give up looking. Clear, audio-friendly design helped; cluttered layouts hurt my engagement.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's exploring, watching videos, participating in discussions, but struggling to actually complete and submit work. You can be highly engaged with learning but still not produce finished assignments. Life circumstances, anxiety, or perfectionism can create that gap between activity and output.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. My exam score was decent even though my assignment completion was low. I studied a lot offline, listened to lectures repeatedly, and prepared for exams in ways that didn't show up in the system. Low visible activity doesn't always mean low learning.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like login frequency and video watching, which are real indicators of presence. But they miss the quality of that engagement and all the offline learning. They can't capture conversations with peers outside the platform or the mental state behind the clicks. It's a partial picture at best.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what I'm learning and feeling connected to the material and people in the course. It's not just logging in; it's thinking about concepts outside of class, discussing with peers, and feeling like the work matters. Engagement is mental and emotional, not just behavioral.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much peer support meant to me emotionally, or how I studied through audio while dealing with sadness. It wouldn't capture the phone calls with classmates that kept me motivated or the offline hours with downloaded materials. The human side of my engagement is invisible to the data.
